intro: The clearest split between Accessibility Insights and Assistiv Labs is cost: Accessibility Insights is free to use, while Assistiv Labs sells a subscription with a free tier (entry price not public). Accessibility Insights is Microsoft's free automated and guided accessibility checks for web and Windows. Assistiv Labs is remote assistive-technology testing platform (screen readers, voice control, switches). Pick Accessibility Insights when the job is developers and qa doing per-page or per-feature accessibility reviews and you accept high lock-in; pick Assistiv Labs when it is distributed qa and a11y engineers testing with screen readers remotely and you accept medium lock-in. The data we have shows Accessibility Insights at fully public pricing and documentation with solid developer experience, and Assistiv Labs at some public pricing / roadmap detail with average developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Accessibility Insights is a poor fit for teams needing continuous monitoring across hundreds of pages, and Assistiv Labs is a poor fit for teams wanting automated wcag scans or monitoring dashboards. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually.
quick_take: Accessibility Insights is for Microsoft's free automated and guided accessibility checks; Assistiv Labs is for remote assistive-technology testing platform (screen readers; decide based on budget vs. support.
verdict_a: Choose Accessibility Insights if cost is the binding constraint and the work maps to Microsoft's free automated and guided accessibility checks for web and Windows.
verdict_b: Choose Assistiv Labs if some ecosystem coupling is acceptable and the work lines up with remote assistive-technology testing platform (screen readers, voice control, switches).

faq.q: Is Accessibility Insights cheaper than Assistiv Labs?
faq.a: Neither Accessibility Insights nor Assistiv Labs publishes entry pricing that lets us compare directly — Accessibility Insights uses a free model and Assistiv Labs uses a subscription model. Get quotes from both before assuming one is cheaper.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Accessibility Insights to Assistiv Labs?
faq.a: Leaving Accessibility Insights for Assistiv Labs is the harder direction: Accessibility Insights is rated high lock-in, so exporting data and rebuilding integrations is the main cost. Going the other way (Assistiv Labs to Accessibility Insights) is usually easier.

Accessibility Insights scores higher on developer experience in our data (4/5 vs 3/5). That reflects public feedback on docs, onboarding, and day-to-day ergonomics. Still, run a spike with your own code — DX ratings don't capture every edge.

faq.q: Is Assistiv Labs a good alternative to Accessibility Insights?
faq.a: Yes — Assistiv Labs is a reasonable alternative to Accessibility Insights for developers and qa doing per-page or per-feature accessibility reviews.. The practical differences are free-vs-subscription billing and high-vs-medium lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Assistiv Labs as a credible swap.
